name: Queue Fundamentals — FIFO, BFS, Implement with Stacks

metadata.career_relevance: Queue is the data structure of BFS — used in shortest path, level-order tree traversal, and task scheduling. 'Implement Queue with Stacks' (LeetCode #232) is a classic interview question. Zerodha's job scheduler processes tasks in FIFO order.

hook.what_you_will_build: Queue operations, BFS level-order traversal, implement queue with two stacks.

visual_aid.description: Queue as horizontal pipe: offer() adds to right (tail), poll() removes from left (head). Arrow 'FIFO'. Beside: BFS level-order on tree. Level 0: root. Level 1: left, right. Level 2: all grandchildren. Queue state at each level shown with size snapshot.
visual_aid.alt_text: Queue FIFO and BFS level-order traversal

worked_example.problem_statement: Implement FIFO queue using only two stacks. Amortized O(1) per operation.

step_by_step_solution.details: inbox stack receives all pushes. On pop: if outbox empty, pour ALL from inbox to outbox (reverses order). Pop from outbox. Amortized O(1): each element transferred at most once.

things_to_notice: Lazy transfer: pour inbox→outbox only when outbox is empty
Each element moves inbox→outbox at most once → amortized O(1)
Amortized O(1) even though individual pop can be O(n)
outbox gives FIFO order (inbox reversed = original push order)

industry_examples.use_case: Job queue processes market data orders FIFO. Queue ensures fair ordering.

applications.interview_relevance: Queue+Stack problems: implement each with the other. BFS template: memorize the while(!q.isEmpty()) + for(size) pattern for level-order.
applications.real_world_workflow: Queue vs Stack: FIFO → Queue (BFS, scheduling). LIFO → Stack (DFS, undo, expression eval).

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Doing BFS but not tracking levels correctly. Snapshot queue size BEFORE the inner loop: `int size=q.size()`. Elements added during the loop are next-level nodes — don't process them this level.
common_gaps.remediation: Pattern: `while(!q.isEmpty()){ int size=q.size(); for(int i=0;i<size;i++){...} }` The size snapshot freezes the number of current-level nodes. Any new offers go to next level.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Level-order traversal mixes levels or loses level information

key_takeaways: Use ArrayDeque for both queue and stack — fastest Java implementation
BFS: queue + size snapshot for level-order
Implement queue with 2 stacks: lazy transfer inbox→outbox, amortized O(1)
Implement stack with 1 queue: rotate queue after each push — O(n) push, O(1) pop

self_check_questions.question: Why is the 'implement queue with two stacks' approach amortized O(1) per operation even though pop() can be O(n)?
self_check_questions.answer: Amortized analysis: each element is moved from inbox to outbox at most once. Suppose we push n elements (n pushes = O(n) total) and then pop all n. The first pop triggers a pour of all n elements from inbox to outbox (O(n) for that one operation). Subsequent n-1 pops are O(1) each (pop directly from outbox). Total cost: n (pushes) + n (pour) + (n-1) (pops) = 3n-1 = O(n) for n+n operations = O(1) amortized per operation. Key: each element traverses the inbox→outbox path exactly once over its lifetime.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Deque & Sliding Window Maximum — the O(n) solution to 'max in every window of size k' using a monotonic deque.
